nativeRange.habitat: Outer reef environments at depths of 40–75 m, generally forming small aggregations near reef structures

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and crevices

diet.notes: Primarily feeds on zooplankton in the wild. In captivity, has been documented accepting frozen mysis shrimp and enriched frozen brine shrimp; may eventually accept quality flake foods in small portions. Multiple small feedings per day are generally advised given the high metabolism typical of anthias. A refugium cultivating copepods and amphipods is commonly considered beneficial as a supplemental food source.

avoidWith: other male conspecifics in the same group

summary: A deep-water Indo-Pacific anthias generally considered one of the more challenging species in its genus, collected from depths around 40–75 m and best kept in small haremic groups with frequent feedings and gradual lighting acclimation.
careNotes:
Pseudanthias lunulatus is a protogynous hermaphrodite — individuals are believed to be born female, with the dominant member of a group capable of transitioning to male. Males tend to display more vivid coloration and elongated caudal fin lobes compared to females.

This species is collected from relatively deep outer reef environments (40–75 m), which means newly acquired specimens have been documented preferring low to moderate lighting initially. Gradual increases in lighting intensity over several weeks are generally advised to support acclimation. Introducing this species before more active or territorial tankmates tends to reduce stress during establishment.

Tangs and other open-water swimmers have been reported as compatible tankmates, while aggressive or large predatory species should generally be avoided.

A tight-fitting lid is advisable, as anthias have been known to jump from open aquaria.

Multiple small feedings (2–4 times daily) are widely recommended given this genus's high metabolic rate. Specimens that refuse prepared foods on arrival may need targeted feeding with live or thawed copepods before transitioning to frozen mysis.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been widely documented for this species. As a protogynous hermaphrodite, sex change is socially mediated — if the dominant male is lost, the largest female in the group has been observed transitioning to replace it. Larval rearing would be expected to be very difficult given the deep-water origin and small larval size typical of anthias.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (8.0 cm SL male/unsexed), temperature range (25.4–28.2°C environmental model), taxonomy (Family Anthiadidae, Order Perciformes), native range (Red Sea, Somalia, Mauritius, Maldives, Bali/Indonesia), and depth range (40–75 m). Reef2Reef wiki returned 403; FishBase served as the primary secondary source.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been widely documented for this species. As a protogynous hermaphrodite, sex change is socially triggered — if the dominant male is removed or dies, the largest female in the group has been observed transitioning to fill that role. Larval rearing is considered very difficult in home aquariums.
